channel.title: Jon Sterling's Forest
channel.description: Recent content on Jon Sterling's Forest

item.title: splitting the generic element of the generic cone
item.description: The underlying function {#p\Sub{I}:I\to I#} of the generic element {#p\Sub{I}:I\multimap I#} of the generic cone {#p\Sub{\bullet}:\Prod{X:\Comma{A}{\UU}}I\multimap X#} under {#I \equiv \widetilde{\SemicohStr}#} has been equipped with the structure of a quasi-idempotent in the sense of Shulman, so we may split it. In particular, we have a type {#J#} together with a retraction {#r : I\to J#} and a section {#s : J \to I#} together with homotopies {#\rho:\TpHtpy{J\to J}{s;r}{\Idn{I}}#} and {#\sigma : \TpHtpy{I\to I}{r;s}{p\Sub{I}}#}.

item.title: identity and composition of reflection algebra homomorphisms
quasi-idempotence of the generic element of the generic cone
a generic cone over the identity functor and its generic element
cones over the identity functor
item.description: Letting {#\UU\in\VV#} be an impredicative pair of universes with {#A:\VV#} a type in the outer universe, our goal is to construct a limit cone over the identity functor in the (∞,1)-category {#\Comma{A}{\UU}#} of reflection algebras in {#\UU#} under {#A:\VV#}, in order to prove that there is an initial reflection algebra. Even though we do not have access to the fully coherent language of (∞,1)-category theory in univalent foundations, we can specify the coherence structure that we need in order to obtain our result.

item.title: alternative presentation of semicoherence structure
path spaces of algebra homomorphisms
item.description: Given a path of reflection algebras {#X,Y:\Comma{A}{\UU}#} and a homomorphism {#f : X\multimap Y#}, the type {#\Sum{g:X\multimap Y}{g\approx f}#} of homomorphisms equipped with a homotopy to {#f#} is contractible, by function extensionality and the characterization of path spaces in dependent sums. Hence it follows that the canonical map {#\TpIdn{X\multimap Y}{f}{g}\to f\approx g#} is an equivalence. TODO: Did I get this right ??

item.title: homotopies between reflection algebra homomorphisms
item.description: Given a pair of reflection algebras {#X,Y:\Comma{\UU}{A}#} and a pair of homomorphisms {#f,g : X\multimap Y#}, a homotopy from {#f#} to {#g#} is defined to be a homotopy {#\phi : Uf\sim Ug#} between the underlying functions together with a dependent homotopy {#\bar{\phi} : \Prod{a:A}\TpIdn{\TpIdn{Y}{f\prn{\eta\Sub{X}a}}{\eta\Sub{Y}a}}{\phi\prn{\eta\Sub{X}a}\bullet \eta\Sub{g}a}{\eta\Sub{f}a}#}. We will write {#f\approx g#} for the type of homotopies from {#f#} to {#g#}. TODO: Did I get this right ??

item.title: semicoherence structure
item.description: Let {#\UU\in\VV#} be an impredicative universe pair with {#A:\VV#} such that {#\IncohNatStr#} is the displayed reflection algebra of incoherent naturality structures over the product {#\Prod{X:\Comma{A}{\UU}}X#} of all reflection algebras. We define a further displayed reflection algebra {#\SemicohStr#} over {#\IncohNatStr#} of “semicoherence structures” associating to each incoherent naturality structure one dimension of coherence data for binary compositions of homomorphisms. In particular, we define {#\SemicohStr#} over {#\widetilde{\IncohNatStr}#} as follows, employing our notion of homotopy between homomorphisms: {## \begin{array}{l} \SemicohStr[u] :\equiv\ \begin{array}{l} \Prod{\brc{X,Y,Z:\Comma{A}{\UU}}} \Prod{f : X\multimap Y} \Prod{g : Y\multimap Z} \Prod{h : X \multimap Z} \Prod{\phi : f;g \approx h}\ \Con{ap}\Sub{g} \prn{\vartheta\Sub{u} f} \bullet \vartheta\Sub{u} g = \phi\prn{\epsilon\Sub{u} X} \bullet \vartheta\Sub{u} h \end{array} \end{array} ##}

item.title: incoherent naturality structure
item.description: Let {#\UU\in\VV#} be an impredicative universe pair with {#A:\VV#} a type in the outer universe, so that we may form the product {#\Prod{X:\Comma{A}{\UU}}X#} of all reflection algebras in {#\UU#} under {#A#}. To express the data of the components of a given {#u:\Prod{X:\Comma{A}{\UU}}#} being preserved by all homorphisms and thus representing “cones” over the identity functor on {#\Comma{A}{\UU}#}, we define a displayed reflection algebra {#\IncohNatStr#} of “incoherent naturality structures” over {#\Prod{X:\Comma{A}{\UU}}{X}#} as follows:

item.title: reflection algebras, homomorphisms, and displays
item.description: Definition (reflection algebras and homomorphisms) [jms-003O] Let \UU be a subuniverse of \VV and let A:\VV be a type. A reflection algebra X under A in \UU is defined to be a type UX:\UU together with a function \Mor{\eta\Sub{X}}{A}{UX} . A homomorphism f of reflection algebras from X to Y is given by a function \Mor{Uf}{UX}{UY} together with a homotopy \eta\Sub{f} : \eta\Sub{Y} \sim Uf\circ \eta_X . When it causes no ambiguity, we will write X:\UU for the underlying type UX of a given reflection algebra X ; likewise, we will write f x rather than Uf x .

item.title: displayed reflection algebras over displayed reflection algebras
item.description: If {#Y#} is a displayed reflection algebra over a reflection algebra {#X:\Comma{A}{\UU}#}, we define a displayed reflection algebra over {#Y#} to be a displayed reflection algebra in the original sense over the total reflection algebra {#\widetilde{Y}#} corresponding to {#Y#}.

item.title: the total reflection algebra
displayed reflection algebra
item.description: Let {#X:\Comma{A}{\UU}#} be a reflection algebra; a displayed reflection algebra {#Y#} over {#X#} is defined to be a dependent type {#\bar{U}Y : X \to \UU#} together with a dependent function {#\bar{\eta}\Sub{Y} : \Prod{a:A}{\bar{U}Y\prn{\eta\Sub{X}a}}.#} We will write {#Y[x]#} for {#\bar{U}Yx#}.

item.title: the product of reflection algebras
item.description: Given a type {#K#} and a family of reflection algebras {#X : K\to \Comma{A}{\UU}#} such that the dependent product {#\Prod{k:K}U\prn{Xk}#} lies in {#\UU#}, we may define the product of product of reflection algebras {#\Prod{k:K}Xk#} to have the following structure map: {## \begin{array}{l} \eta\Sub{\Prod{k:K}Xk} : A \to \Prod{k:K}{Xk}\ \eta\Sub{\Prod{k:K}Xk} a :\equiv \lambda k:K., \eta\Sub{Xk}a \end{array} ##}

item.title: the type of reflection algebras and homomorphisms
item.description: We will write {#\Comma{A}{\UU} \simeq \Sum{X:\UU}{A\to X}#} for the type of reflection algebras under {#A#}; given two reflection algebras {#X,Y:\Comma{A}{\UU}#} we will write {#\prn{X\multimap Y} \simeq \Sum{f:X\to Y} \eta\Sub{Y}\sim f\circ \eta\Sub{X}#} for the type of homomorphisms of reflection algebras.

item.title: reflection algebras and homomorphisms
item.description: Let {#\UU#} be a subuniverse of {#\VV#} and let {#A:\VV#} be a type. A reflection algebra {#X#} under {#A#} in {#\UU#} is defined to be a type {#UX:\UU#} together with a function {#\Mor{\eta\Sub{X}}{A}{UX}#}. A homomorphism {#f#} of reflection algebras from {#X#} to {#Y#} is given by a function {#\Mor{Uf}{UX}{UY}#} together with a homotopy {#\eta\Sub{f} : \eta\Sub{Y} \sim Uf\circ \eta_X#}. When it causes no ambiguity, we will write {#X:\UU#} for the underlying type {#UX#} of a given reflection algebra {#X#}; likewise, we will write {#f x#} rather than {#Uf x#}.

item.title: Idempotents in intensional type theory
item.description: We study idempotents in intensional Martin-Löf type theory, and in particular the question of when and whether they split. We show that in the presence of propositional truncation and Voevodsky’s univalence axiom, there exist idempotents that do not split; thus in plain MLTT not all idempotents can be proven to split. On the other hand, assuming only function extensionality, an idempotent can be split if and only if its witness of idempotency satisfies one extra coherence condition.

item.title: idempotents in wild categories
item.description: The definitions here are adapted from Shulman from the setting of intensional type theory to an arbitrary wild category. Definition (pre-idempotent in a wild category) [jms-003L] Let \CCat be a wild category; a pre-idempotent on an object A:\Ob{\CCat} is defined to be a morphism f:\Hom{\CCat}{A}{A} together with an identification \phi : f;f = f . Definition (quasi-idempotent in a wild category) [jms-003M] Let \CCat be a wild category; a quasi-idempotent on an object A:\Ob{\CCat} is defined to be a pre-idempotent \prn{f,\phi} on A together with an identification \chi between f;\phi : f;\prn{f;f} = f;f and the following composite identification:

item.title: quasi-idempotent in a wild category
item.description: Let {#\CCat#} be a wild category; a quasi-idempotent on an object {#A:\Ob{\CCat}#} is defined to be a pre-idempotent {#\prn{f,\phi}#} on {#A#} together with an identification {#\chi#} between {#f;\phi : f;\prn{f;f} = f;f#} and the following composite identification: {## f;\prn{f;f} \xrightarrow{\Con{assoc}\Sub{\CCat},f,f,f} \prn{f;f};f \xrightarrow{\phi;f} f;f ##}

item.title: pre-idempotent in a wild category
item.description: Let {#\CCat#} be a wild category; a pre-idempotent on an object {#A:\Ob{\CCat}#} is defined to be a morphism {#f:\Hom{\CCat}{A}{A}#} together with an identification {#\phi : f;f = f#}.

item.title: initial objects in wild categories
item.description: Lemma (wild initial objects from cones with trivial generic element) [jms-003B] Let \CCat be a wild category, and let I:\Ob{\CCat} be an object of \CCat . If we may equip a wild natural transformation \Mor{\alpha}{\Delta I}{\Idn{\CCat}} from the constant 0-functor \Mor{\Delta I}{\CCat}{\CCat} to the identity 0-functor \Mor{\Idn{\CCat}}{\CCat}{\CCat} with an identification c_\alpha : \alpha_I = \Idn{I} , then \Hom{\CCat}{I}{A} is contractible for all A:\Ob{\CCat} . (Our proof will follow that of the nLab, which was stated for ordinary categories and functors and natural transformations rather than wild ones.

item.title: wild natural transformations
item.description: Definition (wild natural transformation) [jms-0039] Let \CCat,\DCat be two wild categories, and let F,G:\CCat\to\DCat be two wild 0-functors from \CCat to \DCat . A wild natural transformation is defined by the following data: for each A:\Ob{\CCat} , an element \alpha_{A} : \Hom{\DCat}{\Ob{F}A}{\Ob{G}A} ; for each f:\Hom{\CCat}{A}{B} , an identification \alpha_{f} : \alpha_A; \Homs{G}{f} = \Homs{F}{f};\alpha_B . Definition (wild semi-coherent natural transformations) [jms-003H] Let \CCat,\DCat be two wild categories, and let F,G:\CCat\to\DCat be two wild 1-semi-functors from \CCat to \DCat .

item.title: wild functors
item.description: Definition (wild 0-functor) [jms-0038] Let \CCat and \DCat be two wild categories. A wild 0-functor from \CCat to \DCat is given by the following data: a function \Ob{F} : \Ob{\CCat}\to\Ob{\DCat} ; for each A,B:\Ob{\CCat} , a function \Homs{F} : \Hom{\CCat}{A}{B}\to\Hom{\DCat}{\Ob{F}A}{\Ob{F}B} . In other words, a wild 0-functor is a homomorphism of the underlying graphs. Definition (wild 1-semi-functor) [jms-003F] Let \CCat and \DCat be two wild categories. A wild 1-semi-functor from \CCat to \DCat is given by a wild 0-functor F : \CCat\to\DCat equipped with, for each f:\Hom{\CCat}{C}{D} and g : \Hom{\CCat}{C}{D} , an identification \Con{seq}_F\,f\,g : \Homs{F}\prn{f;g} = \Homs{F}{f};\Homs{G}{g} .

item.title: wild semi-coherent natural transformations
item.description: Let {#\CCat,\DCat#} be two wild categories, and let {#F,G:\CCat\to\DCat#} be two wild 1-semi-functors from {#\CCat#} to {#\DCat#}. A wild semi-coherent natural transformation is defined by the data of a wild natural transformation {#\alpha : F \to G#} together with, for each {#f:\Hom{\CCat}{A}{B}#} and {#g : \Hom{\CCat}{B}{C}#}, a higher identification {#\Con{seq}\Sub{\alpha},f,g#} between {#\alpha\Sub{f;g} : F\prn{f;g};\alpha\Sub{C} = \alpha\Sub{A};G\prn{f;g}#} and the composite identification:

item.title: wild 1-semi-functor
item.description: Let {#\CCat#} and {#\DCat#} be two wild categories. A wild 1-semi-functor from {#\CCat#} to {#\DCat#} is given by a wild 0-functor {#F : \CCat\to\DCat#} equipped with, for each {#f:\Hom{\CCat}{C}{D}#} and {#g : \Hom{\CCat}{C}{D}#}, an identification {#\Con{seq}_F,f,g : \Homs{F}\prn{f;g} = \Homs{F}{f};\Homs{G}{g} #}.

item.title: splitting the generic element of a cone
item.description: Let {#\CCat#} be a wild category, and let {#I:\Ob{\CCat}#} be an object of {#\CCat#} such that {#\Mor{\alpha}{\Delta I}{\Idn{\CCat}}#} is a wild natural transformation from the constant 0-functor {#\Mor{\Delta I}{\CCat}{\CCat}#} to the identity 0-functor {#\Mor{\Idn{\CCat}}{\CCat}{\CCat}#} on {#\CCat#} for some {#I:\Ob{\CCat}#}. Now let {#r:\Hom{\CCat}{I}{J}#} and {#s:\Hom{\CCat}{J}{I}#} be a section-retraction pair witnessed by {#\eta : s;r = \Idn{I}#}, and let {#\sigma : r;s=\alpha_{I}#} be a splitting of {#\alpha_{I}:\Hom{\CCat}{I}{I}#}. Then we may define a wild natural transformation {#\Mor{\beta}{\Delta J}{\Idn{\CCat}}#} and an identification {#\chi : \beta_{J} = \Idn{J}#}.

item.title: the wild constant functor
the wild identity functor
item.description: Let {#\CCat#} be a wild category; the identity 0-functor {#\Mor{\Idn{\CCat}}{\CCat}{\CCat}#} on {#\CCat#} is defined as follows: {## \begin{aligned} \Ob{\Idn{\CCat}}A &:\equiv A\ \Homs{\Idn{\CCat}}f &:\equiv f \end{aligned} ##} The above may be extended to a wild 1-semi-functor as follows: {## \Con{seq}_{F},f,g :\equiv \Refl ##}

item.title: wild initial objects from cones with trivial generic element
item.description: Let {#\CCat#} be a wild category, and let {#I:\Ob{\CCat}#} be an object of {#\CCat#}. If we may equip a wild natural transformation {#\Mor{\alpha}{\Delta I}{\Idn{\CCat}}#} from the constant 0-functor {#\Mor{\Delta I}{\CCat}{\CCat}#} to the identity 0-functor {#\Mor{\Idn{\CCat}}{\CCat}{\CCat}#} with an identification {#c_\alpha : \alpha_I = \Idn{I}#}, then {#\Hom{\CCat}{I}{A}#} is contractible for all {#A:\Ob{\CCat}#}. (Our proof will follow that of the nLab, which was stated for ordinary categories and functors and natural transformations rather than wild ones.

item.title: wild category theory
item.description: We describe some basic definitions and results in “wild (incoherent) category theory”, i.e. the study of untruncated category-like objects in univalent foundations that may lack higher coherences and saturation/completeness assumptions. Some of this is based on the Homotopy Type Theory Coq library, as well as ideas from a blog post of Mike Shulman and private conversations with Mike Shulman. Definition (wild category) [jms-0037] A wild category is given by the following data:

item.title: wild natural transformation
item.description: Let {#\CCat,\DCat#} be two wild categories, and let {#F,G:\CCat\to\DCat#} be two wild 0-functors from {#\CCat#} to {#\DCat#}. A wild natural transformation is defined by the following data: for each {#A:\Ob{\CCat}#}, an element {#\alpha_{A} : \Hom{\DCat}{\Ob{F}A}{\Ob{G}A}#}; for each {#f:\Hom{\CCat}{A}{B}#}, an identification {#\alpha_{f} : \alpha_A; \Homs{G}{f} = \Homs{F}{f};\alpha_B#}.

item.title: wild 0-functor
item.description: Let {#\CCat#} and {#\DCat#} be two wild categories. A wild 0-functor from {#\CCat#} to {#\DCat#} is given by the following data: a function {#\Ob{F} : \Ob{\CCat}\to\Ob{\DCat}#}; for each {#A,B:\Ob{\CCat}#}, a function {#\Homs{F} : \Hom{\CCat}{A}{B}\to\Hom{\DCat}{\Ob{F}A}{\Ob{F}B}#}. In other words, a wild 0-functor is a homomorphism of the underlying graphs.

item.title: wild category
item.description: A wild category is given by the following data: a type {#\Ob{\CCat}#}; for each {#A,B:\Ob{\CCat}#}, a family of types {#\Hom{\CCat}{A}{B}#}; for each {#A:\Ob{\CCat}#}, an element {#\Idn{A} : \Hom{\CCat}{A}{A}#}; for each {#f:\Hom{\CCat}{A}{B}#} and {#g:\Hom{\CCat}{B}{C}#}, an element {#\prn{f;g} : \Hom{\CCat}{A}{C}#} for each {#f:\Hom{\CCat}{A}{B}#}, a identification {#\Con{lunit}\Sub{\CCat} f : \prn{\Idn{A};f} = f#} and a identification {#\Con{runit}\Sub{\CCat} f : \prn{f;\Idn{B}}=f#}; for each {#f:\Hom{\CCat}{A}{B}#} and {#g:\Hom{\CCat}{B}{C}#} and {#h:\Hom{\CCat}{C}{D}#}, a identification {#\Con{assoc}\Sub{\CCat},f,g,h : f;\prn{g;h} = \prn{f;g};h#}.

item.title: reflectivity of impredicative universes
item.description: Working in univalent foundations, let {#\UU\in\VV#} be a pair of universes such that {#\UU#} is closed under {#\VV#}-small products of {#\UU#}-small types; then the subuniverse inclusion {#\Mor{j}{\UU}{\VV}#} is reflective. This argument will combine ideas due to Awodey, Frey, Speight, and Shulman. For any type {#A:\VV#}, we will define the reflection of {#A#} in {#\UU#} in several steps. Reflection Algebras, Homomorphisms, and Displays [jms-003U] Definition (reflection algebras and homomorphisms) [jms-003O] Let \UU be a subuniverse of \VV and let A:\VV be a type.

item.title: the opposite of a cloven fibration
item.description: Let {#E#} be a cloven fibration over {#B#}; in this case, we may use the cleaving of {#E#} to give a simple construction of the opposite fibration {#\OpCat{E}#}. A displayed object of {#\OpCat{E}#} over {#x\in B#} is given by an object of {#E#} over {#x#}. Given {#\Mor{f}{x}{u}\in B#}, a displayed morphism {#\bar{x}\DispTo{f}\bar{y}#} in {#\OpCat{E}#} is given by a vertical map {#\DispMor{h}{\Idn{x}}{\InvImg{f}\bar{y}}{\bar{x}}#}.

item.title: cleaving the family fibration
item.description: The family fibration can be cloven, constructively. In particular, let {#C#} be a category and consider the family fibration {#\FAM{C}#} over {#\SET#}; let {#\Mor{f}{T}{S}#} be a function between sets and let {#u\in C^S#} be an {#S#}-indexed family of objects of {#C#}. The family {#\InvImg{f}u = f;u\in C^T#} is the object part of a cartesian lift for {#u#} along {#f#}. The cartesian morphism {#\DispMor{\bar{f}}{f}{\InvImg{f}u}{u}\in\FAM{C}#} is given by the identity map {#\Mor{\Idn{\InvImg{f}u}}{\InvImg{f}u}{\InvImg{f}u}\in C^T#}.

item.title: cleavings from the axiom of choice
item.description: Assuming the axiom of choice, any cartesian fibration may be equipped with a (non-canonical) cleaving. In the current version of these notes, we freely use this principle, but in the future we would like to follow Ahrens and Lumsdaine in distinguishing between weak and strong fibrations, in which the latter come equipped with cleavings.

item.title: cleaving of a displayed category
item.description: Let {#E#} be a displayed category over {#C#}; following Ahrens and Lumsdaine, we define a cleaving of {#E#} to be a function assigning to each {#\Mor{f}{x}{y}\in C#} and {#u\in C_y#} a cartesian morphism {#\DispMor{\bar{f}}{f}{\InvImg{f}u}{u}#} in {#E#} called the chosen cartesian lift of {#u#} along {#f#}.

item.title: carrable map
item.description: A morphism {#\Mor{p}{E}{B}#} in a category {#\mathcal{C}#} is said to be carrable when for any {#\Mor{f}{A}{B}#}, the pullback {#\Mor{f^*p}{E\times_{B}A}{A}#} depicted below exists.

item.title: exponentiable object
item.description: Let {#E#} be an object in a category {#\mathcal{C}#} such that all binary cartesian products with {#E#} exist. We say that {#E#} is exponentiable when the product endofunctor {#-\times E#} on {#\mathcal{C}#} has a right adjoint, which we shall write {#E\Rightarrow-#}.

item.title: exponentiable arrow
item.description: A carrable map {#\Mor{p}{E}{B}#} in a category {#\mathcal{C}#} is said to be exponentiable when it is exponentiable when viewed as an object of the slice category {#\Sl{\mathcal{C}}{B}#}.

item.title: category with representable maps
item.description: A category with representable maps or CwR is defined by Uemura to be a category {#\mathcal{C}#} with finite limits equipped with a pullback-stable class {#\Repr{\mathcal{C}}\subseteq \Homs{\mathcal{C}}#} of exponentiable arrows in {#\mathcal{C}#}.

item.title: evergreen notes in mathematics
item.description: Definition (evergreen notes) [tfmt-0003] The phrase evergreen note is due to Andy Matuschak, who has written extensively about it in his public Zettelkasten. Evergreen notes are permanent notes that evolve and accumulate over time, cutting across different projects. Atomicity of Mathematical Notes [tfmt-0007] One of the design principles for evergreen notes described by Matuschak is atomicity (Evergreen notes should be atomic): a note should capture just one thing, and if possible, all of that thing.

item.title: forests of evergreen notes
item.description: Definition (forest and trees of evergreen notes) [tfmt-000R] A forest of evergreen notes (or a forest for short) is loosely defined to be a collection of evergreen notes in which multiple hierarchical structures are allowed to emerge and evolve over time. Concretely, one note may contextualize several other notes via transclusion within its textual structure; in the context of a forest, we refer to an individual note as a tree.

item.title: the extent of a tree in a forest
item.description: The extent of a tree within a forest is the smallest set of trees closed under the following rules: {#T#} lies within the extent of {#T#}. If {#S#} is transcluded by {#T#}, then any tree in the extent of {#S#} lies also in the extent of {#T#}.

item.title: contributor to a tree
item.description: A contributor to a tree within a forest is someone who is an author of at least one tree lying within its extent.

item.title: author of a tree
item.description: An author of a tree within a forest is someone who satisfies the following conditions: They contributed intellectually to the immediate textual content of the tree, i.e. the non-transcluded content. They can be held responsible for all the content within the tree, i.e. both the immediate textual content as well as the content of all the subtrees.

item.title: forest and trees of evergreen notes
item.description: A forest of evergreen notes (or a forest for short) is loosely defined to be a collection of evergreen notes in which multiple hierarchical structures are allowed to emerge and evolve over time. Concretely, one note may contextualize several other notes via transclusion within its textual structure; in the context of a forest, we refer to an individual note as a tree. Of course, a tree can be viewed as a forest that has a root node.

item.title: authorship and responsibility in forests
item.description: A forest of evergreen notes may in general contain the work of many authors: for example, the tree on constructive domain theory comprises mathematical contributions of not only the present author, but also Martín Escardó and Tom de Jong. However, the correct attribution of authorship to a given tree is more subtle than one might at first think. To understand this subtlety, we first consider that each individual tree may contain both textual content and transcluded subtrees.

item.title: competing terminologies for cartesian maps
item.description: Some authors including Grothendieck give an equivalent definition of cartesian fibration that factors through a nonequivalent definition of cartesian morphisms. Such authors refer to our notion of cartesian morphism as “hypercartesian” (see Streicher).

item.title: the apartness relation
item.description: Let {#x,y:D#} be two elements of a dcpo {#D#}. We say that {#x#} is apart from {#y#}, written {#x\Apart y#}, if there exists a Scott-open subset {#U\subseteq D#} that contains one but not both of {#x#} and {#y#}. (Adapted from de Jong.)

item.title: positivity and the strictly-below relation
item.description: It follows from our characterization of positivity in terms of partial elements that an element {#a: A#} of a pointed dcpo {#A#} is positive if and only if it is strictly above the bottom element, i.e. {#\bot\StBelow a#}. (See also Proposition 6.2.12 of Tom de Jong’s thesis.)

item.title: way-below vs. positivity
item.description: At first glance, the definitions of the way-below relation and positivity appear very similar: Definition (the way-below relation) [jms-002V] Let x,y:D be two elements of a dcpo D ; we say that x is way below y , written x\WayBelow y , when for any directed subset I\subseteq D such that y\sqsubseteq \bigsqcup{I} , there exists z\in I such that x\sqsubseteq z . (Adapted from Tom de Jong’s thesis.) Definition (the positive elements of a pointed dcpo) [jms-001M] An element u of a pointed dpco A is called positive when for any semidirected subset I\subseteq A , if u\sqsubseteq \bigsqcup{I} then I is inhabited.

item.title: the strictly-below relation
item.description: Let {#x\sqsubseteq y:D#} be two elements of a dcpo {#D#}; we say that {#x#} is strictly below {#y#}, written {#x\StBelow y#}, if whenever {#y\sqsubseteq\bigsqcup_{1+\phi}\brk{x\mid y}#} we have {#\phi=\top#}. (Adapted from Tom de Jong’s thesis.)

item.title: the way-below relation
item.description: Let {#x,y:D#} be two elements of a dcpo {#D#}; we say that {#x#} is way below {#y#}, written {#x\WayBelow y#}, when for any directed subset {#I\subseteq D#} such that {#y\sqsubseteq \bigsqcup{I}#}, there exists {#z\in I#} such that {#x\sqsubseteq z#}. (Adapted from Tom de Jong’s thesis.)

item.title: results in constructive monad theory
item.description: In this section, we gather some useful definitions and results concerning creation of (co)limits. The reason for stating these things explicitly is that the usual definitions of created (co)limits are deeply irrational in a variety of incompatible ways, so it is important to fix definitions that can work from the start. It would be good to reformulate this in terms of displayed categories of algebras. Definition (creation of (co)limits) [jms-001H] Let \Mor{U}{\CCat}{\DCat} be a functor and let \ICat be a category.

item.title: characterization of positivity in terms of partial elements
item.description: Let {#A#} be a pointed dcpo. Then an element {#a:A#} is positive if and only if for any partial element {#u: LA#} such that {#a\sqsubseteq\bigsqcup_{p:u{\downarrow}}u[p]#}, we have {#u{\downarrow}=\top#}. Proof. Therefore, we first suppose that any {#u: LA#} is defined supposing {#a\sqsubseteq\bigsqcup_{p:u{\downarrow}}u[p]#} to check that {#a#} is positive. Fixing a semidirected subset {#I\subseteq A#} such that {#a\sqsubseteq \bigsqcup{I}#}, we must show that {#I#} is inhabited. We consider the partial element {#u=\prn{\exists i\in I.

item.title: the two notions of positivity coincide for pointed dcpos
item.description: Let {#A#} be a pointed dcpo. Then an element {#x:A#} is a positive element of the pointed dcpo if and only if {#\Mor{x}{\ObjTerm{}}{A}#} is a positive element in the dominion {#\mathcal{O}#} of Scott-open immersions of dcpos. Proof. We first observe that the latter condition is equivalent to the following: For any {#u\in LA#}, if {#a\sqsubseteq \bigsqcup_{p: u{\downarrow}}u[p]#} then {#u{\downarrow}=\top#}. This follows from the (constructive) characterization of the path order in dcpos.

item.title: positive elements in a dominion
item.description: Let {#\CCat#} be category with pullbacks, a terminal object, and an initial object, equipped with a dominion {#\mathcal{M}#} that has a Sierpiński interval {#\prn{\Sigma,\top,\bot}#}, such that {#\mathcal{M}#}-partial map classifiers {#LA#} exist for all {#A\in\CCat#}. We will write {#\mathbb{L}=\prn{L,\eta,\mu}#} for the resulting partial map classifier monad on {#\CCat#}. Let {#\prn{A,\alpha}#} be an {#\mathbb{L}#}-algebra in {#\CCat#}, and let {#\Mor{x}{C}{A}#} be a generalized element of {#A#}. We say that {#\Mor{x}{C}{A}#} is positive when for any {#\Mor{u}{C}{LA}#} such that there exists a path from {#\Mor{x}{C}{A}#} to {#\Mor{u;\alpha}{C}{A}#}, then {#\Mor{u}{C}{LA}#} factors through some (necessarily unique) {#\Mor{y}{C}{A}#} as below:

item.title: paths in a dominion
item.description: Let {#\CCat#} be category with pullbacks, a terminal object, and an initial object, equipped with a dominion {#\mathcal{M}#} that has a Sierpiński interval {#\prn{\Sigma,\top,\bot}#}. A path from {#\Mor{f}{I}{C}#} to {#\Mor{g}{I}{C}#} in {#\CCat#} is defined to be a morphism {#\Mor{p}{\Sigma\times I}{C}#} such that the following diagram commutes: We shall write {#p:f\leadsto g#} to denote a path from {#f#} to {#g#}.

item.title: the Sierpiński interval of a dominion
item.description: Let {#\CCat#} be a category with pullbacks, a terminal object and let {#\mathcal{M}#} be a dominion on {#\CCat#}. A Sierpiński space for {#\mathcal{M}#} is defined to be a universal {#\mathcal{M}#}-partial map, i.e. a morphism {#\Mor{\top}{\ObjTerm{\CCat}}{\Sigma}\in\mathcal{M}#} such that for any {#\Mor{i}{U}{A}\in \mathcal{M}#} there exists a unique map {#\Mor{\chi_i}{A}{\Sigma}#} making the following square cartesian: When {#\CCat#} has an initial object {#\ObjInit{\CCat}#} and every {#\Mor{}{\ObjInit{\CCat}}{A}#} lies in {#\mathcal{M}#}, the Sierpiński space becomes an “interval object” {#\prn{\Sigma,\top,\bot}#} where {#\Mor{\bot}{\ObjTerm{\CCat}}{\Sigma}#} is given as follows:

item.title: a general characterization of positivity
item.description: So far we have studied positivity as an order-theoretic property of domains; in this section, we explore a non-order-theoretic generalization of this condition that is applicable in the setting of axiomatic domain theory. This notion is inspired by the alternative characterization positivity in terms of partial elements. Definition (the Sierpiński interval of a dominion) [jms-002P] Let \CCat be a category with pullbacks, a terminal object and let \mathcal{M} be a dominion on \CCat .

item.title: positively generated elements are non-negatively generated
item.description: Let {#a#} be a positively generated element of a pointed dcpo {#A#}. Then {#a#} is also non-negatively generated. Proof. We must check that {#a\sqsubseteq \bigsqcup_{p:\lnot\prn{a = \bot}}a#}. By assumption, it suffices to check that {#\bigsqcup_{p:\IsPos,a}a\sqsubseteq\bigsqcup_{p:\lnot\prn{a=\bot}}a#}, which follows because the bottom element is not positive.

item.title: positively generated basis yields positive generation
item.description: Let {#A#} be a pointed continuous dcpo presented by a basis {#B\subseteq A#} whose elements are all positively generated in {#A#}. Then {#A#} is positively generated. Proof. As positive generation implies non-negative generation for an element of a pointed dcpo, we may conclude that {#A#} is positively generated.

item.title: when is a continuous pointed dcpo positively generated?
item.description: We describe some light conditions under which a continuous dcpo is positively generated. Lemma (non-negatively generated basis yields positive generation) [jms-0026] Let A be a pointed continuous dcpo presented by a basis B\subseteq A such that each element of B is non-negatively generated in A . Then A is positively generated. Proof. We must show that for any a\in A , we have a \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup_{p:\IsPos,a}a . Let B_a be the subset \brc{b \in B \mid b\WayBelow a} .

item.title: basis with decidable bottomhood yields positive generation
item.description: Let {#A#} be a pointed continuous dcpo presented by a basis {#B\subseteq A#} for each element {#b\in B#} it is decidable whether {#b=\bot#}. Then {#A#} is positively generated. Proof. We observe that every element of {#B#} is non-negatively generated, so we may conclude that {#A#} is positively generated.

item.title: non-negatively generated elements
item.description: An element {#a#} of a pointed dcpo {#A#} is called non-negatively generated when {#a \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup_{\lnot\prn{a=\bot}}a#} holds.

item.title: bottom and non-bottom elements are non-negatively generated
item.description: In a pointed dcpo, any non-bottom element is non-negatively generated. The bottom element {#\bot#}. Any compact element {#a:A#} for which {#\lnot\prn{a=\bot}#} holds. Hence, any element {#a#} for which it is decidable whether {#a=\bot#} is non-negatively generated. Proof. The bottom element is clearly non-negatively generated, as it lies beneath any element. Now fix {#a:A#} and assume {#\lnot\prn{a=\bot}#}; we must check that {#a \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup_{p:\lnot\prn{a=\bot}}a#} but the latter is equal to {#a#}.

item.title: the bottom element is not positive
item.description: The bottom element {#\bot#} of a pointed dcpo {#A#} is not positive. Proof. Suppose that {#\bot#} was positive. The empty set {#\brc{}\subseteq A#} is semidirected and {#\bot\sqsubseteq \bigsqcup\brc{}=\bot#}, but nonetheless {#\brc{}#} is not inhabited.

item.title: the positively generated core of a dcpo
item.description: Let {#A#} be a pointed dcpo; the subposet {#\PGCore{A}\subseteq{A}#} spanned by positively generated elements is closed under directed suprema, and thus gives rise to a subdcpo that we shall refer to as the positively generated core of {#A#}. Proof. Let {#I\subseteq A#} be a directed subset whose elements are all positively generated. We must check that the supremum {#\bigsqcup{I}#} is positively generated, i.e. {#\bigsqcup{I} \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup_{p:\IsPos,\bigsqcup{I}}\bigsqcup{I}#}. Fixing {#i\in I#}, we must check that {#i\sqsubseteq \bigsqcup_{p:\IsPos,{\bigsqcup{I}}}\bigsqcup{I}#}.

item.title: positively generated elements
item.description: Let {#A#} be a dcpo; then an element {#a:A#} is called positively generated when it is the supremum of the semidirected subset {#\brc{b:A\mid b=a\land \IsPos,{a}}#}, i.e. we have {#a \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup_{p:\IsPos,a}a#}.

item.title: characterizing freeness in terms of positivity
item.description: In classical mathematics, every {#\LiftM#}-algebra is free; this does not appear to be asserted by constructive mathematics, but in this section we verify that freeness can be characterized in terms of positivity. The main result is that a pointed dcpo {#A#} is positively generated if and only the evident map {#\Mor{}{\Lift{A^+}}{A}#} is an isomorphism. Construction (the “purification” map) [jms-0021] Let A be a pointed dcpo with an open positivity predicate; we define the “purification” map \Mor{\Purify_A}{A}{\Lift{A^+}} into the lift of the subdcpo of positive elements to send a\in A to the partial element \prn{\IsPos\,{a},\lambda p.

item.title: positive generation and open positivity predicates
item.description: In a lifted dcpo {#\Lift{A}#}, the positive elements form an open subdcpo {#\prn{\Lift{A}}^+\cong A#}: thus, it follows that positivity is always an open property in classical mathematics. On the other hand, it seems that constructive mathematics does not necessarily assert that the positive elements form an open subspace in general. In this section, we grapple with this subtlety by studying the relationship between openness of {#A^+#} and a desirable property that we shall refer to as positive generation, which states that every element of {#A#} is the supremum of its “positive part”.

item.title: basic properties of positivity
item.description: Definition (the positive elements of a pointed dcpo) [jms-001M] An element u of a pointed dpco A is called positive when for any semidirected subset I\subseteq A , if u\sqsubseteq \bigsqcup{I} then I is inhabited. We will write \IsPos\,{u} to mean that u is a positive element. (This is adapted from Johnstone’s definition of positive elements of frames to the case of dcpos by Tom de Jong.) Lemma (the bottom element is not positive) [jms-002H] The bottom element \bot of a pointed dcpo A is not positive.

item.title: liftedness = positive generation
item.description: A pointed dcpo {#A#} is lifted if and only if it is positively generated. Proof. This follows from two existing lemmas: Lifted dcpos (free algebras) are positively generated. Positively generated pointed dcpos are free.

item.title: positively generated pointed dcpos are free
item.description: Let {#A#} be a positively generated pointed dcpo; then {#A#} is canonically free on its subdcpo of positive elements {#A^+#}. More specifically, the embedding-projection pair {#\prn{\Lift{\iota^+};\alpha_A}\dashv\Mor{\Purify_A}{A}{\Lift{A^+}}#} given by the purification map is an isomorphism of dcpos. Proof. First, we recall that the embedding-projection pair {#\prn{\Lift{\iota^+};\alpha_A}\dashv\Purify_A#} exists because {#A#} has an open positivity predicate by virtue of its positive generation. Therefore, the statement of this lemma is well-formed. Because {#\prn{\Lift{\iota^+};\alpha_A}\dashv\Purify_A#} is an embedding-projection pair, it remains only to check that {#\Purify_A;\Lift{\iota^+};\alpha_A#} is an inflation, i.

item.title: positivity is upward-closed
item.description: Let {#i\sqsubseteq j#} in a pointed dcpo {#A#} such that {#i#} is positive; then {#j#} is positive. Proof. Fixing semidirected {#J\subseteq A#} such that {#j\sqsubseteq \bigsqcup{J}#}, we must show that {#J#} is inhabited. This follows from our assumption that {#i#} is positive becuase {#i\sqsubseteq j\sqsubseteq \bigsqcup{J}#}.

item.title: the positive part of a semidirected subset is semidirected
item.description: Let {#I\subseteq A#} be a semidirected subset of a pointed dcpo {#A#}; then the intersection {#I \cap A^+#} is semidirected, where {#A^+#} is the subdcpo spanned by positive elements. Proof. Let {#i,j#} be two positive elements of {#I#}; we must show that there exists a positive element {#k\in I#} such that {#i\sqsubseteq k#} and {#j\sqsubseteq k#}. This follows immediately from the directedness of {#I#} and the fact that positivity is monotone.

item.title: positively generated dcpos have open positivity predicates
item.description: Let {#A#} be a positively generated pointed dcpo. Then {#A#} has an open positivity predicate. Proof. Let {#I\subseteq A#} be a directed subset such that {#\bigsqcup{I}#} is positive; we must find a positive element of {#I#}. Because {#A#} is positively generated we know that {#\bigsqcup{I} \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup\prn{I\cap A^+}#}. Because {#\bigsqcup{I}#} is positive and {#I\cap A^+#} is semidirected, it follows that the latter is inhabited.

item.title: non-negatively generated basis yields positive generation
item.description: Let {#A#} be a pointed continuous dcpo presented by a basis {#B\subseteq A#} such that each element of {#B#} is non-negatively generated in {#A#}. Then {#A#} is positively generated. Proof. We must show that for any {#a\in A#}, we have {#a \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup_{p:\IsPos,a}a#}. Let {#B_a#} be the subset {#\brc{b \in B \mid b\WayBelow a}#}. By assumption, it suffices to check that {#\bigsqcup B_a \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup_{p:\IsPos,a}a#}. Fixing {#b\in B_a#}, we must check that {#b\sqsubseteq \bigsqcup_{p:\IsPos,a}a#}.

item.title: lifted dcpos are positively generated
item.description: Let {#A#} be a dcpo; then the lift {#\Lift{A}#} is positively generated. Proof. Let {#u=\prn{\phi,a}#} be an element of {#\Lift{A}#}. Note that {#u#} is equal to {#\bigsqcup_{p:\phi}u#}; as definedness coincides with positivity in lifted dcpos, we have {#\phi = \IsPos,u#}, and so we are done.

item.title: lifted dcpos have open positivity predicates
item.description: Let {#A#} be a dcpo; then the lifting {#\Lift{A}#} has an open positivity predicate. Proof. It suffices to recall that a lifted dcpo is positively generated.

item.title: positively generated dcpos
item.description: When a pointed dcpo {#A#} satisfies either of the following equivalent conditions, we will refer to it as positively generated. Every element {#a: A#} is positively generated, i.e. we have {#a = \bigsqcup_{p:\IsPos,a}a#}. For any semidirected subset {#I\subseteq A#}, we have {#\bigsqcup I = \bigsqcup \brc{a\in I \mid \IsPos,a}#}. Proof. (2) implies (1) by setting {#I = \brc{a}#}. Conversely, assume that each element is the supremum of its positive part and fix a semidirected subset {#I\subseteq A#}.

item.title: open positivity predicate
item.description: We say that a pointed dcpo {#A#} has an open positivity predicate if and only if any of the following equivalent conditions hold: The positivity predicate {#\Mor{\IsPos}{\Gamma A}{\Omega}#} tracks a continuous morphism of dcpos {#\Mor{\IsPos}{A}{\Sigma}#}. The embedding {#\Mor{\iota^+}{A^+}{A}#} is a Scott-open immersion. For a directed subset {#I\subseteq A#}, if {#\bigsqcup{A}#} is positive then there exists an element {#a\in I#} that is positive in {#A#}.

item.title: the "purification" map
pointed dcpos with open positivity predicates are almost free
item.description: Let {#A#} be a pointed dcpo with an open positivity predicate; then the purification map {#\Mor{\Purify_A}{A}{\Lift{A^+}}#} is the projection part of an embedding-projection pair {#\prn{\Lift{\iota^+};\alpha_A}\dashv \Purify_A#}. Proof. We fix {#\prn{\phi,a^+}: \Lift{A^+}#} and compute explicitly using the fact that {#\Purify_A#} preserves the bottom element and therefore preserves semidirected subsets: {## \begin{aligned} \Purify_A\prn{\alpha_A\prn{\Lift{\iota^+}\prn{\phi,a^+}}} &= \Purify_A\prn{\alpha_A\prn{\phi,a^+}} \ &= \Purify_A\bigsqcup_{p:\phi} a^+p \ &= \bigsqcup_{p:\phi} \Purify_A\prn{a^+p} \ &= \bigsqcup_{p:\phi} \bigsqcup_{q:\IsPos\prn{a^+p}} a^+p \ &= \bigsqcup_{p:\phi} a^+p \ &= \prn{\phi,a^+} \end{aligned} ##}

item.title: monadicity of pointed dcpos
item.description: The category of {#\LiftM#}-algebras and homomorphisms is (canonically) equivalent to the category of pointed dcpos and bottom-preserving morphisms of dcpos. It follows that pointed dcpos are monadic over dcpos. Proof. Having a bottom element and being an algebra are both properties of dcpos, because these structures are uniquely determined. Therefore, we will argue that pointed dcpos with bottom-preserving morphisms form the same subcategory of {#\DCPO#} as the category of algebras with algebra-preserving maps.

item.title: bottom-preserving maps vs. algebra morphisms
item.description: Let {#\prn{A,\alpha}#} and {#\prn{B,\beta}#} be two {#\LiftM#}-algebras; then a morphism of dcpos {#\Mor{f}{A}{B}#} is a homomorphism of algebras if and only if it preserves the bottom element. Proof. It is clear that a homomorphism of algebras must preserve the bottom element. On the other hand, suppose that {#\Mor{f}{A}{B}#} preserves the bottom element to check that the following diagram commutes: Because both composites preserve the bottom element, it is enough to observe that they both take {#u\in TA#} to the supremum of the following semidirected subset:

item.title: pointed dcpos and lift-algebras
item.description: Definition (pointed dcpo) [jms-001S] A dcpo is called pointed when it has a bottom element \bot under the information order. Lemma (suprema of semidirected subsets in pointed dcpos) [jms-001U] A dcpo A is pointed if and only if it is closed under suprema of semidirected subsets. Proof. Suppose that A is closed under suprema of semidirected subsets. In particular, we may consider the supremum \bigsqcup\brc{} of the empty set. This is evidently the bottom element, using the universal property of the supremum.

item.title: lift-algebras are pointed
item.description: Let {#\prn{A,\alpha}#} be a {#\LiftM#}-algebra; then the underlying dcpo {#A#} is pointed. Proof. To define the bottom element of {#A#}, we choose {#\alpha\prn{\bot, \lambda_.\star}#}. Fixing {#n\in A#}, we must check that {#\alpha\prn{\bot, \lambda_.\star}\sqsubseteq n#}. Because {#\Lift#} is a Kock–Zöberlein monad and thus we have {#\alpha\dashv\eta_{A}#}, it is equivalent to observe that {#\prn{\bot, \lambda_.\star}\sqsubseteq \eta_{A}n#}.

item.title: suprema of semidirected subsets in pointed dcpos
item.description: A dcpo {#A#} is pointed if and only if it is closed under suprema of semidirected subsets. Proof. Suppose that {#A#} is closed under suprema of semidirected subsets. In particular, we may consider the supremum {#\bigsqcup\brc{}#} of the empty set. This is evidently the bottom element, using the universal property of the supremum. Conversely, suppose that {#A#} is pointed and let {#I\subseteq A#} be semidirected. We may replace {#I\subseteq A#} by the directed subset {#I’ = I\cup \brc{\bot}#}; the inclusion {#I\subseteq I’#} is clearly cofinal, so the supremum of {#I’#} is also the supremum of {#I#}.

item.title: pointed dcpos are lift-algebras
item.description: A pointed dcpo carries a (unique) {#\LiftM#}-algebra structure. Proof. The algebra structure is unique if it exists because {#\LiftM#} is a Kock–Zöberlein monad, i.e. a monad for which the structure maps of algebras are left adjoint to the unit in the order-enrichment. Therefore, it suffices to show that there merely exists an algebra structure {#\Mor{\alpha}{\Lift{A}}{A}#} for any pointed dcpo {#A#}. We define the structure map {#\alpha#} to take {#u\in\Lift{A}#} to the supremum of the semidirected subset {#\brc{x\in A \mid u = \eta_Ax}#}, so we may write {#\alpha u = \bigsqcup_{p:u{\downarrow}}u[p]#}.

item.title: pointed dcpo
item.description: A dcpo is called pointed when it has a bottom element {#\bot#} under the information order.

item.title: cocompleteness of pointed dcpos
item.description: We will deduce the closure of pointed dcpos under connected colimits using general results about monads. If we can show the closure of lifted dcpos under coproducts, we will have established the cocompleteness of the category of pointed dcpos and bottom-preserving maps. Lemma (lifting as a co-comma construction) [jms-001D] For any dcpo A , the following is a co-comma square in the posetal 2-category of dcpos, where \Lift takes a dcpo to its lift or Scott-open partial map classifier.

item.title: positivity in constructive domain theory
item.description: These notes were prepared by Jonathan Sterling, based in part on discussions with Martín Escardó and Tom de Jong. Any errors belong to Jonathan Sterling. Basic Properties of Positivity [jms-002C] Definition (the positive elements of a pointed dcpo) [jms-001M] An element u of a pointed dpco A is called positive when for any semidirected subset I\subseteq A , if u\sqsubseteq \bigsqcup{I} then I is inhabited. We will write \IsPos\,{u} to mean that u is a positive element.

item.title: the subdcpo spanned by positive elements
item.description: Let {#A#} be a pointed dcpo; then the subposet {#A^+\subseteq A#} spanned by positive is a dcpo. Proof. This follows from the closure of positive elements in an algebra under directed suprema.

item.title: closure of positivity under directed suprema
item.description: Let {#A#} be a pointed dcpo; the positivity predicate {#\Mor{\IsPos}{\Gamma A}{\Omega}#} is closed under directed suprema. Proof. Let {#J\subseteq A#} be a directed subset such that every {#u\in J#} is positive; we must show that {#\bigsqcup{J}#} is positive. Fixing a subdirected subset {#I\subseteq A#} such that {#\bigsqcup{J}\sqsubseteq \bigsqcup I#}, we must check that {#I#} is inhabited. The inequality {#\bigsqcup{J}\sqsubseteq \bigsqcup I#} holds if and only if {#u\sqsubseteq \bigsqcup{I}#} for all {#u\in u#}; as {#J#} is directed, we therefore have some positive {#u\in A#} such that {#u\sqsubseteq \bigsqcup{I}#} from which it follows that {#I#} is inhabited.

item.title: positivity = definedness in a lifted dcpo
item.description: The positive elements of a lifted dcpo {#\Lift{A}#} are exactly the defined elements, i.e. those lying in the image of {#\Mor{\eta}{A}{\Lift{A}}#}. Proof. Let {#a\in A#} be an element of the dcpo {#A#}. To show that {#\eta_A a#} is positive, we fix a semidirected subset {#U\subseteq \Lift{A}#} such that {#\eta_A a\sqsubseteq \bigsqcup U#} to show that {#U#} is inhabited. The inequality implies that {#\top\sqsubseteq \exists u\in U. u{\downarrow}#}, so we know that {#U#} is inhabited.

item.title: the positive elements of a pointed dcpo
item.description: An element {#u#} of a pointed dpco {#A#} is called positive when for any semidirected subset {#I\subseteq A#}, if {#u\sqsubseteq \bigsqcup{I}#} then {#I#} is inhabited. We will write {#\IsPos,{u}#} to mean that {#u#} is a positive element. (This is adapted from Johnstone’s definition of positive elements of frames to the case of dcpos by Tom de Jong.)

item.title: connected colimits of lift-algebras
item.description: Let {#\LiftM=\prn{\Lift,\eta,\mu}#} be the lifting monad on dcpos; the Eilenberg–Moore category {#\LiftAlg#} is closed under connected colimits, and these colimits are created by the forgetful functor {#\Mor{\ULiftM}{\LiftAlg}{\DCPO}#}. Proof. This follows from the following facts: The category of dcpos is (even constructively) known to be cocomplete. Lifting preserves connected colimits. The forgetful functor {#\Mor{\ULiftM}{\LiftAlg}{\DCPO}#} creates whatever colimits {#\Lift#} preserves.

item.title: algebra structure on the colimit apex
item.description: Let {#\CCat#} be a category and let {#\TMon = \prn{T,\eta,\mu}#} be a monad on {#\CCat#}, writing {#\Mor{U}{\EM}{\CCat}#} for the forgetful functor from the Eilenberg–Moore category {#\EM#}. Suppose that {#T#} preserves colimits of {#\ICat#}-figures for a given small category {#\ICat#}. Let {#\Mor{X_\bullet}{\ICat}{\EM}#} be a diagram in {#\EM#} such that {#\ICat\xrightarrow{X_\bullet}\EM\xrightarrow{U}\CCat#} has a universal cocone {#\Mor{c_\bullet}{UX_\bullet}{\brc{C}}#}. We may lift {#C#} to an essentially unique {#\TMon#}-algebra structure {#\bar{C}#} with {#U\bar{C}=C#} in a canonical way such that {#\Mor{c_\bullet}{UX_\bullet}{\brc{C}}#} lifts to a cocone of algebras {#\Mor{\bar{c}\Sub{\bullet}}{X\Sub{\bullet}}{\bar{C}}#} with {#U\bar{c}\bullet=c\bullet#}.

item.title: creation of colimits by the forgetful functor
item.description: Let {#\CCat#} be a category and let {#\TMon = \prn{T,\eta,\mu}#} be a monad on {#\CCat#}, writing {#\Mor{U}{\EM}{\CCat}#} for the forgetful functor from the Eilenberg–Moore category {#\EM#}. If {#T#} preserves colimits of {#\ICat#}-figures for a given small category {#\ICat#}, then {#\Mor{U}{\EM}{\CCat}#} creates colimits of {#\ICat#}-figures. Proof. Let {#\Mor{X_\bullet}{\ICat}{\EM}#} be a diagram such that {#\ICat\xrightarrow{X_\bullet}\EM\xrightarrow{U}\CCat#} has a (co)limit cocone {#\Mor{c_\bullet}{UX_\bullet}{\brc{C}}#}. Let {#\bar{C}=\prn{C,\alpha_{\bar{C}}}#} be the canonical algebra structure on the colimit apex {#C#} such that {#\Mor{c_\bullet}{UX_\bullet}{\brc{C}}#} lifts to a cocone of algebras.

item.title: the disorder of created limits
item.description: No two authors use the same definition of created limit. One issue is strictness (old-fashioned definitions are overly strict and refer to equality of objects), but there also appear to be a number of variations with differences of a more substantive nature. My advice is to always check what definition some result is using before trusting it or reusing it, because this is an easy way to sneak an enormous mistake into your work.

item.title: creation of (co)limits
item.description: Let {#\Mor{U}{\CCat}{\DCat}#} be a functor and let {#\ICat#} be a category. The functor {#U#} is said to create (co)limits of {#\ICat#}-figures when for any diagram {#\Mor{C_\bullet}{\ICat}{\CCat}#} such that {#\ICat\xrightarrow{C_\bullet}\CCat\xrightarrow{F}\DCat#} has a (co)limit, then {#C_\bullet#} has a (co)limit that is both preserved and reflected by {#F#}.

item.title: reflection of colimits by the forgetful functor
item.description: Let {#\CCat#} be a category and let {#\TMon = \prn{T,\eta,\mu}#} be a monad on {#\CCat#}, writing {#\Mor{U}{\EM}{\CCat}#} for the forgetful functor from the Eilenberg–Moore category {#\EM#}. If {#T#} preserves colimits of {#\ICat#}-figures for a given indexing category {#\ICat#}, then {#\Mor{U}{\EM}{\CCat}#} reflects colimits of {#\ICat#}-figures. Proof. Let {#\Mor{X_\bullet}{\ICat}{\EM}#} be a diagram in {#\EM#} and let {#\Mor{y_\bullet}{X_\bullet}{\brc{Y}}#} be a cocone in {#\EM#} such that {#\Mor{Uy_\bullet}{UX_\bullet}{\brc{UY}}#} is a universal cocone for {#UX_\bullet = \ICat\xrightarrow{X_\bullet}\EM\xrightarrow{U}\CCat#}.

item.title: lifting preserves connected colimits
item.description: Let {#A_\bullet#} be a diagram of dcpos indexed in a connected category {#\ICat#}. Then the lifting endofunctor {#\Lift#} on dcpos preserves the colimit of {#A_\bullet#}. Proof. Let {#A_\bullet\xrightarrow{\iota_\bullet}\brc{A_\infty}#} be a universal cocone; we must show that {#\Lift{A_\bullet}\xrightarrow{\Lift{\iota_\bullet}}\brc{\Lift{A_\infty}}#} is universal as well, i.e. show any cocone {#\Lift{A_\bullet}\xrightarrow{d_\bullet}\brc{D}#} factors uniquely through {#\Lift{A_\bullet}\xrightarrow{\Lift{\iota_\bullet}}\brc{\Lift{A_\infty}}#} in the following sense: Using the universal property of {#\Lift{A_\infty}#} as a co-comma dcpo, we may define {#\Lift{A_\infty}\xrightarrow{d_\infty}D#} to be the universal map determined by a certain lax square in the following configuration:

item.title: constructive domain theory
item.description: I am accumulating an understanding of constructive domain theory — that is, the domain theory that can be carried out in an elementary topos with a natural numbers object. These notes are based on the work of Tom de Jong and Martín Escardó; these notes, however, assume the propositional resizing principle whereas de Jong and Escardó explore a predicative version domain theory based on large and locally small domains. Results in Constructive Monad Theory [jms-002U] In this section, we gather some useful definitions and results concerning creation of (co)limits.

item.title: lifting as a co-comma construction
item.description: For any dcpo {#A#}, the following is a co-comma square in the posetal 2-category of dcpos, where {#\Lift#} takes a dcpo to its lift or Scott-open partial map classifier. Proof. We fix another lax square of the following form: We must show that there exists a unique morphism {#\Lift{A}\xrightarrow{d}D#} factoring the square above through {#\brc{d_\bot\sqsubseteq d_A}#} as follows: We will define {#\Lift{A}\xrightarrow{d}D#} to take {#a\Sup{?}:\Lift{A}#} to the least upper bound of the directed subset {#U = \brc{d_\bot}\cup \brc{d_A a\mid a\Sup{?

item.title: Domain Theory in Constructive and Predicative Univalent Foundations
item.description: We develop domain theory in constructive and predicative univalent foundations (also known as homotopy type theory). That we work predicatively means that we do not assume Voevodsky’s propositional resizing axioms. Our work is constructive in the sense that we do not rely on excluded middle or the axiom of (countable) choice. Domain theory studies so-called directed complete posets (dcpos) and Scott continuous maps between them and has applications in programming language semantics, higher-type computability and topology.

item.title: § 8.3: information-flow and noninterference
item.description: Finally, an application of synthetic Tait computability to security and information flow control is identified: a security class is a phase distinction between low and high security. The preliminary results presented in this section have been substantially improved upon by Sterling and Harper, adding support for general recursion and termination-insensitive noninterference by combining synthetic Tait computability with synthetic domain theory.

item.title: § 8.2: type refinements and program extraction
item.description: The modal language of synthetic Tait computability promises a new and more abstract account of refinement types and program extraction via a phase distinction between computation and specification. Refinement types are often thought of as a kind of subtype, but there is a fundamental difference: when {#\Rfn{\phi}{A}#} and {#\Rfn{\psi}{B}#} are refinements of types {#A#} and {#B#} respectively, then {#\phi\to\psi#} refines {#A\to B#}. In contrast, subtyping laws for function spaces are contravariant in the domain.

item.title: § 8.1: two phase distinctions for program modules
item.description: A brief overview is given of the applications of synthetic Tait computability to program modules, material that is published in the Journal of the ACM.

item.title: chapter 8: a plan for PL
item.description: This dissertation has focused almost solely on the development and applications of synthetic Tait computability in the context of pure type theory, but the author originally invented synthetic Tait computability to solve problems in core programming languages, as part of Sterling and Harper’s re-analysis of the phase distinction in ML-style module systems between static (compiletime) and dynamic (runtime) code. The purpose of this chapter is to identify several applications of synthetic Tait computability to core programming languages, and set an agenda for future work — some of which has been executed and published following the completion of this dissertation

item.title: part V: prospects
item.description: Chapter 8: A Plan for PL [jms-0019] This dissertation has focused almost solely on the development and applications of synthetic Tait computability in the context of pure type theory, but the author originally invented synthetic Tait computability to solve problems in core programming languages, as part of Sterling and Harper’s re-analysis of the phase distinction in ML-style module systems between static (compiletime) and dynamic (runtime) code. The purpose of this chapter is to identify several applications of synthetic Tait computability to core programming languages, and set an agenda for future work — some of which has been executed and published following the completion of this dissertation

item.title: chapter 6: cartesian cubical type theory
item.description: This expository chapter introduces cubical type theory as an extension to Martin-Löf’s type theory by an interval {#\II#} with two distinct endpoints {#0, 1 : \II#}. The interval is a basic “figure” that defines a notion of path or identification {#\TpPath{A}{u}{v}#} between two elements of any type; for instance, to identify {#u,v:A#} is the same as to construct a function {#p:\II\to A#} such that {#p0 = u#} and {#p1=v#}. Terms involving variables of type {#\II#} can exhibit complex computational behavior that is difficult to account for: for instance, if {#p:\TpPath{A}{u}{v}#} and {#i:\II#} are variables, then the application {#pi:A#} is a normal form, but it must nonetheless reduce to either {#u#} or {#v#} when {#i#} is substituted for by a constant.

item.title: part IV: cubical type theory
item.description: Chapter 6: Cartesian Cubical Type Theory [jms-0017] This expository chapter introduces cubical type theory as an extension to Martin-Löf’s type theory by an interval \II with two distinct endpoints 0, 1 : \II . The interval is a basic “figure” that defines a notion of path or identification \TpPath{A}{u}{v} between two elements of any type; for instance, to identify u,v:A is the same as to construct a function p:\II\to A such that p0 = u and p1=v .

item.title: chapter 5: synthetic normalization by evaluation
item.description: This chapter develops a more sophisticated application of synthetic Tait computability, the proof of normalization and decidability of Martin-Löf’s type theory with a cumulative hierarchy of universes. The synthetic argument contained in this chapter builds on the work of Fiore on categorical normalization by gluing for simply typed λ-calculus, and that of Coquand on a presheaf-theoretic version of normalization by evaluation for dependent types. Analogous to the external argument of Fiore, we construe the syntax of normal and neutral forms as the initial algebra for an internal inductive definition in the language of synthetic Tait computability.

item.title: chapter 7: normalization for cubical type theory
item.description: This chapter reports the main result of the dissertation, normalization for cubical type theory and its corollaries: injectivity of type constructors, and decidability of equality & typing. These results were first obtained by Sterling and Angiuli for the fragment of cubical type theory without universes; the present chapter extends the results of op. cit. to support a cumulative hierarchy of universes. The central innovation of this chapter is to generalize the notion of neutral form to accommodate the computational behavior of terms that have free variables of type {#\II#} discussed in our synopsis of Chapter 6.

item.title: chapter 4: Tait's method of computability
item.description: It is simple enough to verify negative properties of a formal system, e.g. the non-derivability of a given assertion {#\Phi#}: find a mathematical object that models all the rules of the formal system and yet refutes {#\Phi#}. In contrast, it is much harder to verify any non-trivial positive property of a formal system (such as canonicity, normalization, decidability, etc.). To handle such results, new techniques were needed — and delivered in the late 1960s by Tait, Martin-Löf and others under the name of Tait’s method of computability or logical predicates.

item.title: part III: synthetic Tait computability
item.description: Chapter 4: Tait’s Method of Computability [jms-0013] It is simple enough to verify negative properties of a formal system, e.g. the non-derivability of a given assertion \Phi : find a mathematical object that models all the rules of the formal system and yet refutes \Phi . In contrast, it is much harder to verify any non-trivial positive property of a formal system (such as canonicity, normalization, decidability, etc.). To handle such results, new techniques were needed — and delivered in the late 1960s by Tait, Martin-Löf and others under the name of Tait’s method of computability or logical predicates.

item.title: chapter 3: the theory of universes
item.description: Universes started their life in the Grothendieck school of algebraic geometry as a technical device to circumvent the annoyance that there cannot be a “set of all sets”; a universe is a set of enough sets, and whilst a universe cannot contain itself, it may nonetheless lie within an even bigger universe. Several important developments in the theory of universes from the 1970s onward by Bénabou, Martin-Löf, Dybjer, Hofmann, Streicher, Awodey, and others have collided to deliver the present-day understanding of the centrality of universes in semantics: a model of type theory is just a special kind of universe in a special kind of category.

item.title: chapter 2: the language of topoi
item.description: Much of this dissertation is stated and proved by exploiting the language of (Grothendieck) topoi, a concept that leads a dual life as a kind of generalized logic and as a kind of generalized topology. The viewpoint of topos theory qua generalized topology plays an important role in this dissertation, and yet it nonetheless remains unfamiliar to most computer scientists and logicians. For this reason, Chapter 2 is provided as sufficient exposition to understand the use of topoi in the remainder of the dissertation, focusing on the recollement or gluing of a topos from a pair of complementary open and closed subtopoi, a classical construction that provides the geometrical basis for synthetic Tait computability.

item.title: part II: mathematical Background
item.description: Chapter 2: The Language of Topoi [jms-0010] Much of this dissertation is stated and proved by exploiting the language of (Grothendieck) topoi, a concept that leads a dual life as a kind of generalized logic and as a kind of generalized topology. The viewpoint of topos theory qua generalized topology plays an important role in this dissertation, and yet it nonetheless remains unfamiliar to most computer scientists and logicians. For this reason, Chapter 2 is provided as sufficient exposition to understand the use of topoi in the remainder of the dissertation, focusing on the recollement or gluing of a topos from a pair of complementary open and closed subtopoi, a classical construction that provides the geometrical basis for synthetic Tait computability.

item.title: chapter 1: objective syntax of dependent types
item.description: To state and prove theorems like canonicity and decidability for a type theory, we must have a mathematical definition of the syntax of type theory. Conventionally, the syntax of type theory has been studied in several layers: one starts with a definition of “raw” syntax as trees labeled by the names of the generating operations, quotients these trees under permutation of bound variables, and then layers on top of this an additional inductively defined formalism expressing the well-formedness of types, well-formedness of terms, definitional equality of types, and definitional equality of terms.

item.title: chapter 0: conspectus on type theory
item.description: This chapter situates the motivations and applications of type theory in mathematics and computer science, and poses these against the semantic and syntactic properties of type theory that are needed to substantiate these applications. On the semantic side, type theory needs a number of properties including function extensionality, function comprehension, propositional univalence, effective quotients, etc.; on the syntactic side, type theory needs to be at least consistent, and many applications require both canonicity and decidability.

item.title: part I: dependent dype theory
item.description: Chapter 0: Conspectus on Type Theory [jms-000X] This chapter situates the motivations and applications of type theory in mathematics and computer science, and poses these against the semantic and syntactic properties of type theory that are needed to substantiate these applications. On the semantic side, type theory needs a number of properties including function extensionality, function comprehension, propositional univalence, effective quotients, etc.; on the syntactic side, type theory needs to be at least consistent, and many applications require both canonicity and decidability.

item.title: detailed overview of *First Steps in Synthetic Tait Computability*
item.description: We give a chapter-by-chapter overview of First Steps in Synthetic Tait Computability. The novel contributions are contained in Chapters 4, 5, 7, and 8; the remaining Chapters 0-3 and 6 are primarily expository. Part I: Dependent Dype Theory [jms-000W] Chapter 0: Conspectus on Type Theory [jms-000X] This chapter situates the motivations and applications of type theory in mathematics and computer science, and poses these against the semantic and syntactic properties of type theory that are needed to substantiate these applications.

item.title: contributions of *First Steps in Synthetic Tait Computability*
item.description: This dissertation positively resolves the decidability conjecture for cubical type theory, the last remaining open question in its syntactic metatheory. Standard techniques proved inadequate for tackling this problem, so the bulk of this dissertation focuses on developing a new mathematical technique called synthetic Tait computability that generalizes and abstracts the method of Tait computability or logical predicates; in the past two years, synthetic Tait computability has played a central role in solving several problems in both type theory and core programming languages, suggesting that this dissertation presents a lasting and transformative contribution to the state of the art.

item.title: cubical canonicity
item.description: For any closed term {#\cdot\vdash N:\TpNat#} of cubical type theory, there exists a unique natural number {#n\in\Nat#} such that {#\cdot\vdash N\equiv \EncodeNum{n}:\TpNat#} where {#\EncodeNum{n}#} is the encoding of the number {#n#} as a term in the type theory.

item.title: decidability of cubical type theory
item.description: The assertions {#\Gamma\vdash \IsTp{A}#}, {#\Gamma\vdash\IsTp{A\equiv B}#}, {#\Gamma\vdash M:A#}, and {#\Gamma\vdash M\equiv N: A#} are all decidable.

item.title: background on homotopy and cubical type theory
item.description: For more than four decades, dependent type theory has been positioned as the “common language” that can finally unify mathematics and computer programming: while it has never been controversial that a computer program is a form of mathematical construction, the running hypothesis of the type theoretic community has been the converse to this claim, namely that mathematical constructions should be viewed as programs that can in principle be executed by a physical machine — roughly, sets = types and elements = programs.

item.title: dissertation overview: *first steps in synthetic tait computability*
item.description: This is a chapter-by-chapter overview of the dissertation of Jonathan Sterling. Background on Homotopy and Cubical Type Theory [jms-000R] For more than four decades, dependent type theory has been positioned as the “common language” that can finally unify mathematics and computer programming: while it has never been controversial that a computer program is a form of mathematical construction, the running hypothesis of the type theoretic community has been the converse to this claim, namely that mathematical constructions should be viewed as programs that can in principle be executed by a physical machine — roughly, sets = types and elements = programs.

item.title: naïve logical relations in synthetic Tait computability
item.description: Logical relations are the main tool for proving positive properties of logics, type theories, and programming languages: canonicity, decidability, conservativity, computational adequacy, and more. Logical relations combine pure syntax with non-syntactic objects that are parameretized in syntax in a somewhat complex way; the sophistication of possible parameterizations makes logical relations a tool that is primarily accessible to specialists. In the spirit of Halmos’ Naïve Set Theory, we advance a new viewpoint on logical relations based on synthetic Tait computability, an internal language for categories of logical relations.

item.title: using synthetic methods to prove analytical facts
item.description: Today when synthetic methods are employed, it is usually with the intention of proving something about a concrete (analytically constituted) object. For instance, synthetic differential geometry is used to prove things about actual smooth manifolds; synthetic domain theory can be used to prove things about actual {#\omega#}-cpos. Likewise, synthetic Tait computability can be used to prove things about the actual syntax of actual programming languages!

item.title: category theory vs. type theory
item.description: Category theory is the language that is best equipped to express external statements and arguments, because category theory is explicit about the “domain of definition” of a given (generalized) element. In contrast, type theory is the language of internal mathematics; in type theory one works naïvely with “elements” {#x:A#}, and the constraints of the language ensure that everything you do is compatible with these formal elements being realized by generalized elements {#x:I\to A#}.

item.title: well-pointedness vs. function extensionality
item.description: One of the consequences of passing to an intuitionistic set theory is that we must deal with distinctions that did not have significance in the classical setting, such as the failure of well-pointedness. In classical set theory, well-pointedness means that a function {#f : A \to B#} is completely determined by its behavior on global elements {#x\in A#} (i.e. functions {#x : 1\to A#}), whereas in intuitionistic set theory this need not hold (indeed, well-pointedness in this sense implies the law of the excluded middle).

item.title: Euclid's synthetic plane geometry
item.description: The canonical example of the synthetic method is the ancient Greek mathematician Euclid’s plane geometry, which starts from an axiomatization of the properties of points, lines, circles, and angles as primitive notions. For instance: A straight line can be drawn between any two points. A line segment can be extended to a straight line. There exists a circle with any center and radius. All right angles are equal to each other.

item.title: the relationship between internal and external language
item.description: The development of axiomatic set theory followed by the discovery of sheaves and forcing has led to a new style of synthetic mathematics that differs somewhat from Euclid’s. In the new synthetic method, we start from a concrete domain concept (such as topological spaces, smooth manifolds, computational datatypes, homotopy types, etc.) and summon as if from the void a new kind of set theory in which instances of the original domain concept are treated naïvely as though they were just special sets.

item.title: Euclid's parallel postulate
item.description: “If a straight line falling on two straight lines make the interior angles on the same side less than two right angles, the two straight lines, if produced indefinitely, meet on that side on which the angles are less than two right angles.”

item.title: synthesis, analysis, and the axiomatic method
item.description: The axiomatic method in mathematics is a tug of war between analysis and synthesis: analysis studies an object by modeling it with some substance that reliably behaves like the object in question, whereas synthesis takes stock of the competing models for an object and proceeds from an axiomatization of their essential properties. Analysis is important because it allows scientists to distinguish between empty fantasies and concepts that are grounded in reality by proceeding from the general to the particular; synthesis is no less important because it sharpens our view of the objects we study by summating our historical (analytical) experience in the form of axioms.

item.title: enriched category
item.description: Let {#\prn{V,I,\otimes,\alpha,\lambda,\rho}#} be a monoidal category. A category {#C#} enriched in {#V#} is defined by the following structure and axioms: a collection {#\Ob{C}#} of objects; for any two objects {#x,y#}, an object {#\Hom{C}{x}{y}\in V#}; for every object {#x#}, a morphism {#I\xrightarrow{i\Sub{x}}\Hom{C}{x}{x}#} in {#V#}; for all objects {#x,y,z#}, a morphism {#\Hom{C}{x}{y}\otimes\Hom{C}{y}{z}\xrightarrow{c\Sub{x,y,z}}\Hom{C}{x}{z}#} in {#V#}. such that the following diagrams commute in {#V#}:

item.title: monoidal categories are bicategories with a single object
item.description: A monoidal category is nothing more than a bicategory with only a single object; to be more precise, given a such a bicategory {#B#} with just one object {#\bullet\in B#}, the hom category {#\Hom{B}{\bullet}{\bullet}#} is monoidal, with the tensor product given by horizontal composition, the tensor unit given by the horizontal identity, and unitors and associators given by those of {#B#}.

item.title: convention on chirality of unitors
item.description: We always prefer our notation to reflect the diagrammatic order of composition rather than the applicative order, since the latter disrupts geometric intuitions. As a result, what we have called the “left and right” unitors respectively in our definition of bicategories are inverted from the definitions one finds in many other texts.

item.title: bicategory
item.description: A bicategory {#B#} is given by the following structure and axioms: a collection of objects {#x,y,z#} called 0-cells; for each pair of 0-cells {#x,y#} a category {#\Hom{B}{x}{y}#} of morphisms or 1-cells {#x\xrightarrow{f}y#}; for each object {#x#}, a morphism {#x\xrightarrow{\Idn{x}}x#} called the identity morphism; for all objects {#x,y,z#}, a functor {#\Hom{B}{x}{y}\times\Hom{B}{y}{z}\xrightarrow{\prn{\HSeq}}\Hom{B}{x}{z}#} called horizontal composition; for all 1-morphisms {#e\xrightarrow{f}x\xrightarrow{g}y\xrightarrow{h}z#}, an isomorphism {#\prn{f\HSeq g}\HSeq h \xrightarrow{\alpha\Sub{f,g,h}} f\HSeq\prn{g\HSeq h}#} natural in {#f,g,h#} called the associator; for all 1-morphisms {#x\xrightarrow{f}y#}, an isomorphism {#\Idn{x}\HSeq f\xrightarrow{\lambda\Sub{f}} f#} natural in {#f#} called the left unitor; for all 1-morphisms {#x\xrightarrow{f}y#}, an isomorphism {#f\HSeq\Idn{y}\xrightarrow{\rho\Sub{f}} f#} natural in {#f#} called the right unitor; such that the triangle below commutes for all 1-morphisms {#x\xrightarrow{f}y\xrightarrow{g}z#}: and the pentagon below commutes for all 1-morphisms {#v\xrightarrow{e}w\xrightarrow{f}x\xrightarrow{g}y\xrightarrow{h}z#}:

item.title: monoidal category
item.description: A monoidal category is a category {#C#} equipped with the following structure and axioms: a functor {#C\times C\xrightarrow{\otimes}C#} called the tensor product; an object {#I\in C#} called the tensor unit; a natural isomorphism {#\prn{x\otimes y}\otimes z \xrightarrow{\alpha\Sub{x,y,z}} x\otimes\prn{y\otimes z}#} called the associator; a natural isomorphism {#I\otimes x \xrightarrow{\lambda\Sub{x}}x#} called the left unitor; a natural isomorphism {#x\otimes I \xrightarrow{\rho\Sub{x}}x#} called the right unitor; such that the triangle below commutes: and the pentagon below commutes:

item.title: kernels as equalizers
item.description: Evidently, the kernel of a morphism {#A\xrightarrow{f} B#} in a category with zero morphisms is the equalizer of {#A\xrightarrow{f} B#} with the zero morphism {#A\xrightarrow{0} B#}.

item.title: category with zero morphisms
item.description: A category with zero morphisms is defined to be a category enriched in the category of pointed sets and strict maps. The distinguished point of {#\Hom{\ACat}{A}{B}#} shall be written {#0#}.

item.title: kernel
item.description: We will often write {#\Ker{f}\xrightarrow{\InclKer{f}}A#} for the kernel of a map {#A\xrightarrow{f}B#} in a category with zero morphisms {#\ACat#}.

item.title: kernel
item.description: Let {#\ACat#} be a category with zero morphisms. Then the kernel of a morphism {#A\xrightarrow{f} B#} in {#\ACat#} is defined to be a morphism {#K\xrightarrow{k}{A}#} such that for any {#X\xrightarrow{h}A#} with {#h;f=0#}, there exists a unique map {#X\xrightarrow{}K#} factoring {#X\xrightarrow{h}A#} through {#K\xrightarrow{k}{A}#} in the sense of the diagram below:

item.title: monomorphisms in terms of exactness
item.description: A sequence {#\ObjInit{\ACat}\xrightarrow{}A\xrightarrow{f}B#} in an abelian category {#\ACat#} is exact if and only if {#A\xrightarrow{f}B#} is a monomorphism. Proof {#(\Rightarrow)#}. Suppose that {#\ObjInit{\ACat}\xrightarrow{}A\xrightarrow{f}B#} is exact, and fix {#X\xrightarrow{g,h}A#} such that {#g;f = h;f#} to check that {#g=h#}. Considering that {#\ACat#} is abelian, this is the same as to check that {#(g-h);f = 0#} implies {#g-h=0#}. Using our assumption, we may factor {#g-h#} uniquely through the kernel of {#f#} as follows:

item.title: short exact sequences
item.description: A short exact sequence in an abelian category {#\AA#} is an exact sequence of the form {#\ShortSequence{\AA}{A}{f}{B}{g}{C}#}.

item.title: exact sequences and complex sequences
item.description: An exact sequence in an abelian category is a sequence of maps whose interior terms are all exact. A complex sequence is one whose interior terms are all complex.

item.title: exact terms are complex
item.description: If a sequence {#\Sequence{A}{f}{B}{g}{C}#} in an abelian category is exact at {#B#}, then it is complex. Proof. By exactness at {#B#} we may assume {#\Ker{g}=\Img{f}#}; we must show that {#f;g=0#}. Based on our assumption, we see that {#f;g#} factors through the zero morphism as follows: Any morphism factoring through zero must be zero.

item.title: abelian categories
item.description: Some notes on abelian categories, based on my study of Vakil’s The Rising Sea. Definition (exact terms and complex terms) [jms-0002] Let \Sequence{A}{f}{B}{g}{C} be a sequence in an abelian category \ACat . The term B is called complex when f;g=0 , and exact when the kernel of g is isomorphic to the image of f , in other words \Ker{g}=\Img{f} . Definition (exact sequences and complex sequences) [jms-0005] An exact sequence in an abelian category is a sequence of maps whose interior terms are all exact.

item.title: exact terms and complex terms
